title: Laurelin
text:
Preface
Calculus made sense to me in a way that most mathematics did not. Not because it was easy ã it was not ã but because it described things that changed. Velocity and acceleration. The area under a curve. The box dimensions that maximize volume while minimizing material. I remember thinking: if I can write an equation for a falling object, I should be able to write one for sales figures, service orders, or the demand forecasts that actually drive business decisions.
I could not. I did not know where to start.
The equations we worked with in calculus had tidy, well-behaved variables. The variables I cared about ã orders, failures, costs ã did not come pre-packaged as functions. I did not know how to turn a spreadsheet into mathematics. I took a course that covered regression and did not recognize it as the tool I had been looking for. I finished my undergraduate degree in financial engineering with the vague sense that the mathematics I had learned was somehow adjacent to the problems I wanted to solve, but not quite touching them.
That gap stayed with me for a long time.
I went to graduate school in economics knowing almost nothing about the field. What drew me in was the mathematics ã more of it, and different from what I had seen in financial engineering. I had always found it easy to develop an interest in new areas when the underlying structure was rigorous, and economics turned out to have plenty of that. I started working seriously with data ã analysis, visualization, pattern recognition. The original question, the one about describing real business variables with equations, receded into the background. It felt like a different project.
It was not until my PhD that I realized I had been circling the same problem the entire time. Optimization, it turns out, is precisely the discipline of writing equations that describe what we want and finding the inputs that satisfy them. Machine learning is, at its core, the application of that idea to data. The regression I had not recognized as useful in my undergraduate years was the same regression I was now extending, formalizing, and building new variants of for my dissertation.
This book is my attempt to build that connection explicitly ã to draw the straight line between calculus and ML that took me twenty years to see.

The mathematics here start from first principles and build toward the contributions of my PhD thesis: a family of Support Vector Regression models that optimize percentage-error loss functions directly. The early chapters require nothing beyond multivariable calculus. The later chapters are graduate-level, but the goal throughout is the same: to show where the equations come from and why they take the form they do.
Code examples appear in paired R and Python tabs. Advanced derivations are in collapsible sections ã they are there for completeness, but skipping them does not break the argument.

title: 1ô  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong
text:
1.1 A decision you make every day
There are two kinds of forecasters: those who canãt predict the future, and those who donãt know they canãt predict the future.
The first group has an advantage. They stop trying to be right and start trying to be less wrong ã and that turns out to be a problem you can actually solve.
You are running out of coffee at home. You need to decide how many bags to buy. Buy too few and you run out before the week ends. Buy too many and they go stale before you finish them. Neither is catastrophic, but both have a cost.
Without thinking about it, you are solving an optimization problem. You have a loss ã the combined cost of under-buying and over-buying ã and you are trying to minimize it given what you know: your consumption rate, how long bags last, how often you go to the store.
Now scale that up. You are not buying coffee for one person. You are managing the electricity grid for an entire region. Every thirty minutes, you need to decide how much power to procure for the next interval. Buy too little and you impose rolling blackouts. Buy too much and you pay for energy that goes nowhere. The cost of being wrong is not stale coffee ã it is millions of dollars and compromised infrastructure.
The structure of the problem is the same. The stakes are not.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization
1ô  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong

title: 1ô  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong
text:
1.2 Two datasets, one question
This book uses two datasets as its main thread. They come from different domains and have different structures, but they share the same underlying question: given what you can observe, what is your best prediction of what you cannot?

1.2.1 Victorian electricity demand
vic_elec records half-hourly electricity demand and temperature across the state of Victoria, Australia, from 2012 to 2014. Every row is a moment. Every moment is a decision someone had to make about how much power to have available.


Code
library(tsibbledata)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(here)

vic_elec <- readr::read_csv(here("data", "vic_elec.csv"),
                             show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  mutate(Time = as.POSIXct(Time))

vic_sample <- vic_elec |>
  filter(Time >= as.POSIXct("2014-01-01"),
         Time <  as.POSIXct("2014-01-15"))

p_demand <- ggplot(vic_sample, aes(x = Time, y = Demand)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2166ac", linewidth = 0.5) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Demand (MW)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

p_temp <- ggplot(vic_sample, aes(x = Time, y = Temperature)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#d6604d", linewidth = 0.5) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Temperature (ô¯C)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

p_demand / p_temp








Figureô 1.1: Half-hourly electricity demand (top) and temperature (bottom) in Victoria, Australia, January 2014. The relationship is clear but imprecise ã temperature alone does not determine demand.





Temperature drives demand ã air conditioning is the dominant load in an Australian summer. But the relationship shifts. A hot Tuesday looks different from a hot Saturday. A heat wave on the third consecutive day draws more than a heat wave on the first. The signal is real; it is just not simple enough to write down by hand.

1.2.2 California housing prices
The second dataset is 20,640 California census blocks from the early 1990s. Each block is described by eight numbers: median income, house age, average rooms, average bedrooms, population, average occupancy, latitude, longitude. The target is median house value.


Code
library(ggplot2)
library(here)

California <- readr::read_csv(here("data", "california_housing.csv"),
                               show_col_types = FALSE)

ggplot(California, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude,
                       colour = log(median_house_value))) +
  geom_point(size = 0.3, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = "log(value)") +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 1.3) +
  labs(x = "Longitude", y = "Latitude") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)








Figureô 1.2: Median house value across California census blocks. Coastal proximity matters, but income, density, and other variables all play a role. No single feature explains the target.





There is no time axis here. Census blocks do not arrive in sequence ã each one is independent. What matters is not what happened before, but which other blocks are similar and whether similar blocks tend to have similar prices. The coastal premium is visible in the plot. So is the concentration of high-value blocks in the Bay Area and Los Angeles. But the relationship between the eight features and the target is not something you can read off a map.

title: 1ô  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong
section: 1.3 Why a formula is not enough
text:
1.3 Why a formula is not enough
When you write =0.3 * B2 + 85000 in a spreadsheet, you have already made the decision. You chose the slope. You chose the intercept. The formula does what you told it to do, nothing more.
For the electricity problem, you do not know the slope in advance. You do not even know that the relationship is linear. The only honest starting point is the data itself ã and the question of what it means for a prediction to be good.
That question has a precise answer. You choose a way to measure how wrong a prediction is. Then you find the function that is least wrong, on average, over all the data you have. That search is optimization. Every model in this book is the result of that search, with different choices about what ãwrongã means and what functions are allowed.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization
1ô  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong

title: 1ô  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong
text:
1.4 What comes next
Part I builds the optimization machinery from scratch. You will see how to find the minimum of a function when there are no constraints, then when there are. You will learn to read the conditions that tell you whether a solution is optimal. By the end, you will have the tools to derive models from first principles ã not just use them.
Parts II and III introduce the models themselves: how to measure similarity between observations, and how to use that similarity to build predictions that generalize beyond the training data.
The final chapters return to these two datasets with models that optimize percentage error directly ã a choice that matters when a $50,000 prediction error means something very different for a $100,000 house than for a $600,000 house.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization
1ô  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong

title: 1ô  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong
text:
1.5 Exercises






NoteExercise 1.1 ã Reading the time series





Load vic_elec and compute the average half-hourly demand separately for weekdays and weekends. Plot both on the same axis. At what hour of the day is the gap between weekday and weekend demand largest?









NoteExercise 1.2 ã The cost of being wrong





Suppose electricity costs $150 per MWh during peak hours (5ã9 PM on weekdays) and $40 per MWh at all other times. Using vic_elec:

Compute the total procurement cost if you always buy exactly what was demanded.
Compute the cost if your forecast is 10% high during peak hours (you over-procure; the excess is wasted).
Compute the cost if your forecast is 10% low during peak hours (you under-procure; you must buy the deficit at a spot premium of $500 per MWh).

Which direction of error is more expensive? Does your answer change how you would define a good prediction?









NoteExercise 1.3 ã What does ãwrongã mean?





For the California Housing data, compute prediction errors for two naive models: one that always predicts the training mean, and one that always predicts $200,000.
Compute MAE, RMSE, and MAPE for each. Which metric best captures whether the model is useful? Does a $50,000 error mean the same thing for a $100,000 house and a $600,000 house?









NoteExercise 1.4





A loss function \(\ell(\hat{y}, y)\) is scale-free if multiplying both the prediction and the target by the same positive constant leaves the loss unchanged: \(\ell(c\hat{y}, cy) = \ell(\hat{y}, y)\) for all \(c > 0\).
Show that squared error \((\hat{y} - y)^2\) is not scale-free. Then construct a loss function that is. Is it unique? What structural property do all scale-free loss functions share?

title: 2ô  Unconstrained Optimization
text:
2.1 Derivatives and the direction of change
Every warehouse manager knows the feeling. Order too little and you run out of stock mid-week, lose sales, and scramble to reorder at a premium. Order too much and you pay to store inventory nobody needs, watch it depreciate, and tie up capital that could go elsewhere. Somewhere between those two failures is the right number ã and finding it does not require guessing.
The total cost of managing inventory has two competing pieces. Holding cost grows with order quantity: the more units you store, the more you spend on warehouse space, insurance, and opportunity cost. Ordering cost goes the other way: the more you order per shipment, the fewer shipments you make and the less you spend on delivery and setup fees.
Let \(q\) denote the order quantity, \(D\) the annual demand, \(h\) the holding cost per unit per year, and \(s\) the fixed cost per order. The classical Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) model combines these into a total annual cost:
\[
C(q) = \frac{hq}{2} + \frac{Ds}{q},
\qquad q > 0.
\tag{2.1}\]
The first term increases linearly with \(q\); the second decreases hyperbolically. Their sum produces a U-shaped curve with a single minimum ã the kind of structure where calculus is exactly the right tool.
The derivative \(C'(q)\) measures how \(C\) changes when \(q\) changes by an infinitesimal amount. At any point \(q_0\):
\[
C'(q_0) = \lim_{\varepsilon \to 0} \frac{C(q_0 + \varepsilon) -
C(q_0)}{\varepsilon}.
\]
If \(C'(q_0) > 0\), the cost increases as \(q\) increases ã we are to the right of the minimum. If \(C'(q_0) < 0\), it decreases ã we are to the left. The derivative encodes both direction and rate of change.
For the EOQ cost:
\[
C'(q) = \frac{h}{2} - \frac{Ds}{q^2}.
\]
In several variables, the derivative generalizes to the gradient:
\[
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \left[\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1},\
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2},\ \ldots,\ \frac{\partial f}{\partial
x_p}\right]^\top.
\]
The gradient at \(\mathbf{x}_0\) points in the direction of steepest ascent of \(f\) from that point. Its magnitude measures how rapidly \(f\) changes in that direction. This geometric picture is the foundation of every first-order optimization method we will encounter in this book.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 2ô  Unconstrained Optimization
text:
TipThe derivative as slope and direction



Think of the derivative at a point as the slope of the tangent line to the curve at that point. A positive slope means ãgoing uphill to the right.ã A negative slope means ãgoing downhill to the right.ã Zero slope means the tangent is flat ã we are at a critical point.
In multiple dimensions, the gradient \(\nabla f\) generalizes the derivative: it is a vector pointing in the direction of steepest ascent. Moving opposite to the gradient ã downhill ã brings us closer to a minimum.


























Figureô 2.1: EOQ total cost as a function of order quantity. The holding and ordering cost components are shown separately. The minimum occurs where the two components intersect.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 2ô  Unconstrained Optimization
text:
2.2 First-order optimality conditions
The U-shape of \(C(q)\) in Figureô 2.1 reveals the key idea: at the minimum, the curve has zero slope. The holding cost is rising at exactly the rate the ordering cost is falling. Neither term dominates ã they balance.
This balance is not a coincidence. It is a consequence of the first-order optimality condition: at a local minimum \(q^*\) of a differentiable function, the derivative must vanish.






ImportantFirst-order necessary condition



Let \(f : \mathbb{R}^p \to \mathbb{R}\) be differentiable. If \(\mathbf{x}^*\) is a local minimum of \(f\), then
\[
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}.
\]
In one dimension: \(f'(x^*) = 0\).


This condition is necessary but not sufficient on its own ã a zero gradient identifies a candidate, not a confirmed minimum. A maximum and a saddle point also satisfy \(\nabla f = 0\).
For the EOQ cost, setting \(C'(q) = 0\):
\[
\frac{h}{2} - \frac{Ds}{q^2} = 0
\implies
q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2Ds}{h}}.
\tag{2.2}\]
With \(D = 10{,}000\), \(s = 200\), and \(h = 2.5\), this gives \(q^* = \sqrt{1{,}600{,}000} = 1{,}265\) units ã the vertical line in Figureô 2.1.






WarningCommon confusion: necessary ã  sufficient



The condition \(\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}\) is necessary for a local minimum ã but a zero gradient alone does not confirm that you have found one. The function \(f(x) = x^3\) satisfies \(f'(0) = 0\), yet \(x = 0\) is an inflection point, not a minimum. Always verify second-order conditions or use structural properties (such as convexity) to confirm.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 2ô  Unconstrained Optimization
text:
2.3 Critical points and second-order conditions
A point where \(\nabla f = \mathbf{0}\) is called a critical point. Three geometric possibilities exist: a minimum, a maximum, or a saddle point. The second derivative determines which.
In one dimension, the second derivative \(f''(x^*)\) measures how quickly the slope is changing at the critical point:

\(f''(x^*) > 0\): the slope is increasing ã the curve is concave up. The critical point is a local minimum.
\(f''(x^*) < 0\): the slope is decreasing ã the curve is concave down. The critical point is a local maximum.
\(f''(x^*) = 0\): the test is inconclusive. Higher derivatives must be examined.

For the EOQ cost, \(C''(q) = 2Ds/q^3 > 0\) for all \(q > 0\). The critical point at \(q^*\) is confirmed as a minimum.
In multiple variables, the second-order information is encoded in the Hessian matrix \(H_f(\mathbf{x})\), whose \((i,j)\)-th entry is the mixed partial derivative:
\[
[H_f(\mathbf{x})]_{i,j} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i\, \partial x_j}.
\]
The Hessian plays the role of the second derivative: it captures the curvature of \(f\) in every direction simultaneously.






ImportantSecond-order optimality conditions



Let \(\mathbf{x}^*\) satisfy \(\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}\).

Sufficient for local minimum: \(H_f(\mathbf{x}^*)\) is positive definite ã that is, \(\mathbf{v}^\top H_f(\mathbf{x}^*)
\mathbf{v} > 0\) for all \(\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}\).
Sufficient for local maximum: \(H_f(\mathbf{x}^*)\) is negative definite.
Saddle point: \(H_f(\mathbf{x}^*)\) is indefinite ã positive in some directions, negative in others.



Positive definiteness of the Hessian is the multivariable generalization of \(f'' > 0\). Checking it amounts to verifying that all eigenvalues of \(H_f(\mathbf{x}^*)\) are positive ã or equivalently, that all leading principal minors of \(H_f(\mathbf{x}^*)\) are positive (Sylvesterãs criterion).






NoteDerivation: classifying critical points via the Hessian





A quadratic Taylor expansion of \(f\) around a critical point \(\mathbf{x}^*\) gives:
\[
f(\mathbf{x}^* + \mathbf{v}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \underbrace{
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^\top \mathbf{v}}_{=\,0} +
\frac{1}{2}\, \mathbf{v}^\top H_f(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{v}.
\]
The first-order term vanishes at a critical point. The sign of the remainder depends entirely on \(\mathbf{v}^\top H_f(\mathbf{x}^*)
\mathbf{v}\):

Positive for every nonzero \(\mathbf{v}\) (positive definite Hessian) \(\Rightarrow\) \(f(\mathbf{x}^* + \mathbf{v}) > f(\mathbf{x}^*)\) in every direction \(\Rightarrow\) local minimum.
Negative for every nonzero \(\mathbf{v}\) (negative definite Hessian) \(\Rightarrow\) local maximum.
Mixed sign (indefinite Hessian) \(\Rightarrow\) the function increases in some directions and decreases in others from \(\mathbf{x}^*\) \(\Rightarrow\) saddle point.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 2ô  Unconstrained Optimization
text:
2.4 Convexity and global minima
The second-order conditions guarantee a local minimum ã a point better than its immediate neighbors. For optimization in practice, we usually want a global minimum. And local methods, including gradient descent, can get stuck in local minima if the function has the wrong shape.
Convexity is what prevents this.






ImportantConvexity



A function \(f : \mathbb{R}^p \to \mathbb{R}\) is convex if for any two points \(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}\) and any \(\lambda \in [0, 1]\):
\[
f\!\left(\lambda \mathbf{x} + (1-\lambda)\mathbf{y}\right)
\leq \lambda f(\mathbf{x}) + (1-\lambda) f(\mathbf{y}).
\tag{2.3}\]
Geometrically: the function lies below or on the chord connecting any two points on its graph.


This definition has a powerful consequence: a convex function has no local minima that are not also global. If you find a point where \(\nabla f = \mathbf{0}\) and \(f\) is convex, you have found the global minimum ã there is nothing better anywhere.






TipGeometric picture: the bowl



A strictly convex function looks like a bowl. Any ball you place inside rolls to the bottom ã the unique global minimum. A non-convex function has bumps and valleys; the ball may settle in a local depression without ever reaching the lowest point.
For convex functions, the first-order condition \(\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)
= \mathbf{0}\) is not just necessary ã it is also sufficient for a global minimum.


The EOQ cost \(C(q) = hq/2 + Ds/q\) is convex on \(q > 0\): its second derivative \(C''(q) = 2Ds/q^3\) is strictly positive everywhere on the domain. The critical point at \(q^*\) is therefore the unique global minimum. No other order quantity can do better.
Several equivalent conditions characterize convexity:

Via second derivative (one dimension): \(f\) is convex if and only if \(f''(x) \geq 0\) for all \(x\) in the domain.
Via Hessian (multiple dimensions): \(f\) is convex if and only if \(H_f(\mathbf{x})\) is positive semidefinite for all \(\mathbf{x}\) in the domain.
Strict convexity: replace \(\leq\) with \(<\) in Equationô 2.3 (for \(\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{y}\)) ã this guarantees uniqueness of the minimum.







TipWhy convexity matters for optimization



Most objectives we will encounter in this book are convex by construction. This is not accidental ã the models are designed that way deliberately. Convexity guarantees that any point satisfying the first-order conditions is a global minimum, not merely a local one. There is no risk of a solver finding a bad solution that happens to satisfy \(\nabla f = \mathbf{0}\). When we arrive at the optimization problems in Part III, this property will be the reason we can trust the solutions we compute.


The sum of two convex functions is convex, and a nonnegative scalar multiple of a convex function is convex. These preservation rules allow us to certify convexity for complex objectives by decomposing them into simpler pieces.





















Figureô 2.2: Geometric interpretation of convexity. For any two points on the EOQ cost curve, the chord (dashed) lies entirely above the curve ã the defining property of a convex function.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 2ô  Unconstrained Optimization
text:
2.5 Gradient descent: geometric intuition
When a closed-form solution to \(\nabla f = \mathbf{0}\) exists ã as it does for the EOQ ã calculus gives us the answer directly. The derivative equation is solved in closed form, and we are done. But most objective functions encountered in machine learning have no closed-form minimizer. The equation \(\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}\) may be nonlinear or involve thousands of unknowns that must be solved numerically.
Gradient descent is the simplest numerical strategy. Its logic is purely geometric: the gradient \(\nabla f(\mathbf{x})\) points uphill. Moving in the direction \(-\nabla f(\mathbf{x})\) ã the negative gradient ã takes us downhill.
Starting from an initial point \(\mathbf{x}_0\), one gradient descent step produces:
\[
\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 - \eta\, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0),
\tag{2.4}\]
where \(\eta > 0\) is the step size (also called the learning rate). The step size controls how far we move along the downhill direction.
On the EOQ cost, one step from \(q_0 = 2{,}000\) with \(\eta = 0.05\):
\[
C'(2000) = \frac{2.5}{2} - \frac{10000 \times 200}{2000^2} = 1.25 - 0.5 = 0.75.
\]
\[
q_1 = 2000 - 0.05 \times 0.75 = 1999.96.
\]
That is a small step ã the derivative has units of dollars per unit, and \(\eta\) must be calibrated accordingly. The point is not the arithmetic; it is the direction. The gradient told us we were too far right (positive derivative means we should decrease \(q\)), and the step corrects course.






TipWhat gradient descent does and does not do



Gradient descent, as described here, is a geometric idea ã not a complete algorithm. The one-step picture in Equationô 2.4 captures the logic. Repeated application moves steadily toward the minimum.
What we have not specified: how to choose \(\eta\), when to stop, how to handle noisy or high-dimensional settings. Those details matter enormously in practice and are the subject of Chapter 6, which covers first- and second-order numerical methods in full. The purpose here is only geometric: the gradient tells you where uphill is, and you go the other way.























Figureô 2.3: One step of gradient descent on the EOQ cost. Starting at \(q_0 = 2000\), the negative gradient points left ã toward the minimum. The step lands at \(q_1\).
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 2ô  Unconstrained Optimization
text:
2.6 Exercises
Exercise 2.1. A server provisioning problem has the following response time cost:
\[
L(n) = \frac{a}{n} + b \cdot n^2, \qquad n > 0,
\]
where \(n\) is the number of allocated servers, \(a > 0\) captures the throughput benefit of more servers, and \(b > 0\) captures the coordination overhead that grows super-linearly.

Find the first-order optimality condition and solve for \(n^*\).
Verify that \(n^*\) is a minimum using the second-order condition.
Is \(L(n)\) convex on \(n > 0\)? Justify using the second derivative.


Exercise 2.2. Let \(f(x, y) = (x - 2)^2 + (y - 3)^2 + xy\).

Find all critical points by solving \(\nabla f = \mathbf{0}\).
Compute the Hessian \(H_f\) and classify each critical point.
Is \(f\) convex? Provide a criterion-based justification.


Exercise 2.3. The gradient descent update \(x_{t+1} = x_t - \eta f'(x_t)\) applied to \(f(x) = x^2\) starting from \(x_0 = 10\):

Show that after \(t\) steps, \(x_t = x_0 (1 - 2\eta)^t\).
For what range of \(\eta\) does the sequence converge to the minimum?
What happens when \(\eta = 1\)? When \(\eta > 1\)?


Exercise 2.4 ã Graduate level. Consider a function \(f : \mathbb{R}^p \to \mathbb{R}\) that is differentiable and convex.

Prove that any local minimum of \(f\) is also a global minimum. (Hint: suppose \(\mathbf{x}^*\) is a local minimum and \(\mathbf{y}\) satisfies \(f(\mathbf{y}) < f(\mathbf{x}^*)\). Derive a contradiction using the convexity definition.)
Show that if \(f\) is strictly convex, the global minimum is unique.
Now suppose \(f\) is convex and \(\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}\). Prove, using the first-order characterization of convexity (\(f(\mathbf{y}) \geq f(\mathbf{x}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x})^\top
(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x})\)), that \(\mathbf{x}^*\) is a global minimum.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 3ô  Constrained Optimization
text:
3.1 The limits of unconstrained optimization
A portfolio manager has $1,000,000 to invest. She wants to maximize expected return. Left unconstrained, the answer is trivial: borrow as much as possible and put it all in the highest-yielding asset. But she cannot do that. Every peso must be allocated ã no cash sitting idle, no leverage. The constraint changes everything.
In Chapter 2, the recipe was straightforward. Write an objective function, set its gradient to zero, check the second-order conditions. If the function is convex, you have found the global minimum.
That recipe requires nothing of the solution except that it satisfies \(\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0\). The solution can live anywhere in \(\mathbb{R}^p\). When the portfolio manager adds the constraint that allocations must sum to exactly one, she is saying the solution must live somewhere specific ã on a particular surface in the space of all possible portfolios. The unconstrained optimum may not be there.
The figure below illustrates the conflict. The contour lines are the objective; the shaded region is where the solution is allowed to be. The unconstrained minimum sits outside that region. Following the gradient from any point inside the feasible region leads toward that forbidden point.
The gradient does not know about the constraint. It points in the direction of steepest descent, which may be straight out of the region where the solution is allowed to live. A different set of tools is needed ã ones that take the boundary seriously.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 3ô  Constrained Optimization
text: Figureô 3.1: The unconstrained minimum (circle) lies outside the feasible region (shaded). The gradient at any interior point points toward it ã out of bounds. The constrained minimum (triangle) is the closest feasible point to the unconstrained solution.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 3ô  Constrained Optimization
text:
3.2 Feasible regions
A constrained optimization problem has the form
\[
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^p} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, m \\
& h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, \ldots, r
\end{aligned}
\tag{3.1}\]
where \(f\) is the objective, \(g_i\) are inequality constraints, and \(h_j\) are equality constraints. The feasible region \(\mathcal{F}\) is the set of all \(\mathbf{x}\) that satisfy every constraint simultaneously:
\[
\mathcal{F} = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^p : g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0 \text{ for all } i, \; h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0 \text{ for all } j\}.
\tag{3.2}\]
The constrained optimum is the point in \(\mathcal{F}\) where \(f\) is smallest. If \(\mathcal{F}\) is empty ã the constraints are contradictory ã the problem is infeasible and has no solution. If \(f\) is unbounded below on \(\mathcal{F}\), the problem is unbounded.






TipConvexity of the feasible region



When all inequality constraints \(g_i\) are convex functions and all equality constraints \(h_j\) are affine (linear plus constant), the feasible region \(\mathcal{F}\) is a convex set. Convexity of \(\mathcal{F}\) combined with convexity of \(f\) guarantees that any local minimum is a global minimum ã the same guarantee we had in Chapter 2, now carried into the constrained setting.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 3ô  Constrained Optimization
text:
3.3 Equality constraints
The portfolio problem is the cleanest example of an equality constraint. The manager allocates fractions \(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_p\) of her capital across \(p\) assets. The fractions must sum to one: every peso must go somewhere.
\[
x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_p = 1, \quad x_k \geq 0.
\tag{3.3}\]
This is not an approximation or a soft target. It is a hard constraint. Leaving 5% in cash is a choice with a cost ã the opportunity cost of not investing ã and the managerãs mandate does not allow it.
An equality constraint \(h(\mathbf{x}) = 0\) defines a surface in \(\mathbb{R}^p\): all the points where \(h\) evaluates to zero. In two dimensions, it is a curve. The feasible solutions must lie exactly on that curve.
The figure below shows what this means geometrically. The objective has circular contours centered at its unconstrained minimum. The constraint \(h(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 + x_2 - 1 = 0\) is a line. The constrained minimum is the point on the line where the contours are tightest ã where the objective is smallest among all points satisfying the constraint.





















Figureô 3.2: The equality constraint \(h(\mathbf{x}) = 0\) defines a line. The constrained minimum (triangle) is the point on that line closest to the unconstrained minimum ã the contour tangent to the constraint.



The geometric insight is worth holding onto. At the constrained minimum, the contour of \(f\) is tangent to the constraint surface. Moving along the constraint in either direction increases \(f\). This tangency condition ã the contours of \(f\) and the constraint surface sharing the same direction ã is precisely what the Lagrangian captures formally in ?sec-lagrangian.






WarningEquality constraints are rarely met in practice



A constraint like \(\sum_k x_k = 1\) is exact in theory but fragile in numerical optimization. Floating-point arithmetic means the sum may be \(1.0000000003\) rather than exactly \(1\). In practice, equality constraints are enforced up to a tolerance. This matters when implementing solvers ã always check whether your solver requires exact feasibility or accepts an \(\epsilon\)-tolerance.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 3ô  Constrained Optimization
text:
3.4 Inequality constraints
The production planning anchor is more natural for inequalities. A factory has 200 machine-hours available per week and 150 kg of raw material. It produces two goods: tables and chairs. Each table requires 5 machine-hours and 3 kg of material; each chair requires 2 machine-hours and 4 kg. The manager wants to maximize revenue.
\[
\begin{aligned}
\max_{x_1, x_2} \quad & 80 x_1 + 50 x_2 \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 5 x_1 + 2 x_2 \leq 200 \\
& 3 x_1 + 4 x_2 \leq 150 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
\tag{3.4}\]
Each inequality constraint cuts a half-space from \(\mathbb{R}^2\). The feasible region is their intersection ã a polygon whose edges are the constraint boundaries.
Unlike an equality constraint, an inequality constraint does not force the solution to a surface. It rules out one side of a boundary and leaves the other side open. The solution can be anywhere in the interior or on the boundary.





















Figureô 3.3: The feasible region (shaded) is the intersection of all inequality half-spaces. The objective contours increase to the upper right. The constrained optimum (triangle) lies at a vertex ã where two constraints are simultaneously active.



The optimal solution sits at a corner of the feasible region. This is not a coincidence for linear objectives and linear constraints ã it is a theorem (the fundamental theorem of linear programming). For nonlinear objectives and constraints, the optimum can be anywhere on the boundary or in the interior, depending on the problemãs geometry.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 3ô  Constrained Optimization
text:
3.5 Active and inactive constraints
At any feasible point \(\mathbf{x}\), each inequality constraint \(g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0\) is either active (binding) or inactive.
A constraint is active at \(\mathbf{x}\) if \(g_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0\) ã the point sits exactly on the boundary. Moving away from the boundary into the interior relaxes the constraint. A constraint is inactive at \(\mathbf{x}\) if \(g_i(\mathbf{x}) < 0\) ã the point is strictly inside the half-space, and the constraint is not exerting any pressure on the solution.
The distinction matters because inactive constraints are invisible at the optimum. If you removed them, the solution would not change. Active constraints are the ones that actually determine where the optimum is.
In the production example, the machine-hours constraint and the material constraint are both active at the optimum \(\mathbf{x}^* = (250/7, \, 75/7)\): the factory uses exactly 200 machine-hours and exactly 150 kg of material. The non-negativity constraints \(x_1 \geq 0\) and \(x_2 \geq 0\) are inactive ã the factory produces positive quantities of both goods, so those boundaries are not touched.





















Figureô 3.4: Three constraints bound the feasible region (shaded). At the constrained minimum (triangle), only \(g_1 = 0\) is active ã it determines the solution. Constraints \(g_2\) and \(g_3\) are inactive: removing them would not change the optimum.



This observation leads to one of the central ideas in constrained optimization: at the optimum, the active constraints are the ones that matter. An algorithm does not need to track all \(m\) constraints equally ã it needs to identify which ones are binding. This is exactly what the KKT conditions in ?sec-kkt accomplish through the complementary slackness condition.






ImportantActive constraints at the optimum



At the constrained minimum \(\mathbf{x}^*\), each inequality constraint satisfies exactly one of the following:

\(g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0\) ã the constraint is active: it is binding and shapes the solution.
\(g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0\) ã the constraint is inactive: it is satisfied with slack and does not affect the optimum.

The set of active constraints at \(\mathbf{x}^*\) is called the active set \(\mathcal{A}(\mathbf{x}^*) = \{i : g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0\}\).


Equality constraints are always active by definition: \(h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0\) must hold everywhere in \(\mathcal{F}\), including at the optimum.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 3ô  Constrained Optimization
text:
3.6 Exercises
3.1 A company produces two products. Product A requires 3 hours of labor and 2 kg of material per unit; product B requires 1 hour and 4 kg. Available: 120 labor-hours, 160 kg of material. Revenue is $40 per unit of A and $30 per unit of B.

Write the optimization problem in standard form (Equationô 3.1).
Sketch the feasible region. Identify each constraint boundary.
Find all corner points of the feasible region.
At the revenue-maximizing corner, which constraints are active?

3.2 Consider the problem
\[
\min_{x_1, x_2} \; (x_1 - 3)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad x_1 + 2x_2 = 4.
\]

What is the feasible set? Describe it geometrically.
The unconstrained minimum is \((3, 2)\). Is it feasible? Why or why not?
Use geometry to find the constrained minimum without calculus. (Hint: which point on the constraint line is closest to \((3,2)\)?)

3.3 Suppose you add the constraint \(x_1 \leq 10\) to the problem in Exercise 3.2.

Is this constraint active at the solution you found in 3.2c?
Does the solution change? Explain why or why not in terms of the active set.

3.4 (Graduate level) Consider a feasible region defined by \(m\) convex inequality constraints \(g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0\). Show that if each \(g_i\) is a convex function, then \(\mathcal{F}\) is a convex set. (Hint: use the definition of convexity ã for any two feasible points and any \(\lambda \in [0,1]\), the convex combination must also be feasible.)
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 4ô  The Lagrangian and Duality
text:
4.1 Constraints and the feasibility problem
A marketing team has 50,000 pesos to distribute across three channels ã Google Ads, Meta, and email ã and needs to decide how much to allocate to each. The goal is to maximize total conversions. The constraint is exact: the budget must be spent in full, nothing left over, nothing overspent. Chapter 3 gave us the vocabulary for this ã feasible region, constrained optimum, active constraints. What it did not give us is a method for finding that optimum. That is what this chapter is about.
Let \(x_1\), \(x_2\), \(x_3\) denote the amounts allocated to Google Ads, Meta, and email respectively, measured in thousands of pesos. The problem is:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\underset{x_1, x_2, x_3}{\text{maximize}} \quad & f(x_1, x_2, x_3) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 50 \\
& x_1, x_2, x_3 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
\tag{4.1}\]
The objective \(f\) encodes how many conversions each allocation produces. We will specify it shortly. The first constraint is an equality ã the budget is fixed. The three non-negativity conditions are inequalities ã you cannot allocate a negative amount to any channel.
The equality constraint collapses the search space immediately. With \(x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 50\), the three variables are not independent: once we fix \(x_1\) and \(x_2\), the value of \(x_3\) is determined. We are effectively optimizing over a two-dimensional surface embedded in three-dimensional space.
Gradient descent, as introduced in Chapter 2, cannot handle this. It follows \(\nabla f\) without regard for the constraint. A step that increases conversions but violates the budget is not a valid step. We need a way to incorporate the constraint into the optimization itself ã not as a filter applied after the fact, but as a structural component of the problem.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 4ô  The Lagrangian and Duality
text:
TipWhy equality constraints are the harder case



Inequality constraints (\(x_k \geq 0\)) may or may not be active at the optimum. An inactive inequality is irrelevant ã the optimizer ignores it. An equality constraint is always active by definition. The optimum must lie exactly on the constraint surface, which makes equality constraints the governing structure of the problem.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 4ô  The Lagrangian and Duality
section: 4.2 The Lagrangian function and shadow prices
text:
4.2 The Lagrangian function and shadow prices
The key idea is to stop treating the constraint as a barrier and start treating it as a cost. If we penalize violations of \(x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 50\) by an amount \(\lambda\) per unit of violation, the constrained problem becomes an unconstrained one ã at least in form.
The Lagrangian is:
\[
\mathcal{L}(x_1, x_2, x_3, \lambda) = f(x_1, x_2, x_3) - \lambda \left( x_1 + x_2 + x_3 - 50 \right)
\tag{4.2}\]
The scalar \(\lambda\) is the Lagrange multiplier. It enters with a negative sign because we are maximizing: a constraint violation (\(x_1 + x_2 + x_3 > 50\)) reduces the Lagrangian, discouraging infeasible allocations.
At the optimum, the constraint holds exactly ã \(x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 50\) ã so the penalty term is zero and \(\mathcal{L} = f\). But the multiplier has not disappeared. It leaves a trace in the first-order conditions.
Stationarity. At the optimum, \(\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial x_k = 0\) for each \(k\):
\[
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_k} = \lambda, \quad k = 1, 2, 3
\tag{4.3}\]
This is the central insight. At the optimum, the marginal return of every active channel must be equal ã and that common value is \(\lambda\).
Now ask: what happens if the budget increases by one unit, from 50 to 51? The optimum shifts. The gain in objective value from that extra unit of budget is exactly \(\lambda\). This is the shadow price of the budget constraint ã the value of one additional peso.






ImportantShadow price interpretation



If \(\lambda = 3.2\), then each additional thousand pesos of budget yields approximately 3,200 additional conversions at the margin. If \(\lambda = 0.1\), the campaign is already near saturation ã extra budget buys little. The multiplier is not just a mathematical artifact. It is a quantity with direct economic meaning.


This interpretation motivates the entire theory. Lagrange multipliers are not correction terms appended to make the math work. They are the prices of constraints ã the marginal cost of tightening or the marginal benefit of relaxing each one.

4.2.1 Diminishing returns and the shape of \(f\)
The equality constraint forces the budget to be spent in full, but the shape of \(f\) determines how it should be split. Each channel has its own conversion curve ã how many additional conversions a peso buys as spending increases. These curves are concave: the first peso on Google Ads produces more conversions than the thousandth.





















Figureô 4.1: Conversion curves for three marketing channels as a function of budget allocated. Each curve is concave ã the marginal return of an additional peso decreases as the channel becomes saturated. At the optimum, the slopes of all three curves are equal: this common slope is the shadow price \(\lambda\).



Formally, let the total conversion function be:
\[
f(x_1, x_2, x_3) = 80(1 - e^{-0.10\, x_1}) + 60(1 - e^{-0.14\, x_2}) + 40(1 - e^{-0.20\, x_3})
\tag{4.4}\]
The stationarity condition Equationô 4.3 says the marginal return of each channel must equal \(\lambda\) at the optimum. Since the curves have different shapes, this forces different allocations: the channel with the fastest-decaying marginal return receives less budget.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 4ô  The Lagrangian and Duality
text:
4.3 Primal and dual problems
The Lagrangian construction separates the original problem into two perspectives on the same object.
The primal problem is Equationô 4.1 as stated: choose \(x_1, x_2, x_3\) to maximize \(f\) subject to the constraint. The decision variables are the allocations.
The dual problem flips the perspective: choose \(\lambda\) to minimize the maximum of \(\mathcal{L}\) over all \((x_1, x_2, x_3)\). The decision variable is the multiplier. Formally:
\[
\underset{\lambda}{\text{minimize}} \quad g(\lambda) = \underset{x_1, x_2, x_3}{\max} \; \mathcal{L}(x_1, x_2, x_3, \lambda)
\tag{4.5}\]
The function \(g(\lambda)\) is the dual objective. For any fixed \(\lambda\), the inner maximization finds the best unconstrained allocation given that shadow price. The outer minimization over \(\lambda\) finds the shadow price that tightens this upper bound as much as possible.






TipGeometric picture



Think of the Lagrangian surface \(\mathcal{L}(x_1, x_2, x_3, \lambda)\) as a family of unconstrained problems, one per value of \(\lambda\). Each member of the family gives an upper bound on the constrained optimum. The dual problem finds the tightest such bound.


With \(x_3 = 50 - x_1 - x_2\) imposed by the budget constraint, the objective becomes a function of two variables. The surface below shows \(f(x_1, x_2, 50 - x_1 - x_2)\) over the feasible region.















Figureô 4.2: Objective surface \(f(x_1, x_2, 50 - x_1 - x_2)\) over the feasible region, with the budget constraint \(x_3 = 50 - x_1 - x_2\) imposed. The diamond marks the constrained optimum. The surface is strictly concave ã the optimum is unique. At that point, the gradient of \(f\) is not zero; it points off the constraint surface into infeasible territory. The Lagrangian stationarity condition Equationô 4.3 captures optimality on the constraint surface itself.



The surface makes one thing immediate: the unconstrained maximum of \(f\) would be at much higher values of \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) (and correspondingly negative \(x_3\), which is infeasible). The constraint bends the problem. The dual construction makes that bending precise.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 4ô  The Lagrangian and Duality
text:
4.4 Duality and the duality gap
The relationship between primal and dual objectives is governed by two results, one universal and one conditional.
Weak duality holds always: the dual objective upper-bounds the primal objective.
\[
g(\lambda) \geq f^* \quad \text{for all } \lambda
\tag{4.6}\]
where \(f^*\) is the primal optimal value. This follows directly from the definition: for any fixed \(\lambda\) and any feasible \((x_1, x_2, x_3)\),
\[
g(\lambda) = \max_{x} \mathcal{L}(x, \lambda) \geq \mathcal{L}(x^*, \lambda) = f(x^*) - \lambda \cdot 0 = f(x^*)
\]
The constraint is zero at the primal optimum \(x^*\), so the gap between \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(f\) vanishes there.
Strong duality goes further: under the right conditions, there exists a \(\lambda^*\) such that \(g(\lambda^*) = f^*\). The gap closes completely.
\[
g(\lambda^*) = f^*
\tag{4.7}\]
The condition sufficient for strong duality in our setting is Slaterãs condition: there exists a strictly feasible point, and the objective is concave. Both hold here ã the objective Equationô 4.4 is strictly concave (sum of strictly concave functions), and any interior allocation satisfies the budget constraint strictly.






ImportantStrong duality under convexity and Slaterãs condition



If the primal problem is convex (concave objective for maximization, convex feasible region) and Slaterãs condition holds, then strong duality holds: \(g(\lambda^*) = f^*\). The primal and dual optima coincide, and \(\lambda^*\) is the shadow price of the binding constraint.
This result is what makes the dual problem useful in practice: solving the dual gives the primal solution at no extra cost.























Figureô 4.3: Dual objective \(g(\lambda)\) (solid curve) and primal optimum \(f^*\) (dashed line) as a function of the shadow price \(\lambda\), for a single-constraint version of the marketing problem. Weak duality: \(g(\lambda) \geq f^*\) for all \(\lambda\). Strong duality: at \(\lambda^*\), the gap closes exactly ã \(g(\lambda^*) = f^*\). The dual problem amounts to finding \(\lambda^*\).



Two observations from Figureô 4.3 worth internalizing. First, the dual objective is convex in \(\lambda\) ã it has a unique minimum even though the primal is a maximization problem. This is not a coincidence: \(g(\lambda) = \max_x \mathcal{L}(x, \lambda)\) is a pointwise maximum of linear functions of \(\lambda\), which is always convex. Second, the minimum of \(g\) is achieved at exactly the shadow price where the equal-marginal-return condition Equationô 4.3 holds.

4.4.1 Extension to multiple inequality constraints
The equality-constraint treatment extends directly. Suppose the team also has minimum spend requirements per channel: at least 5,000 pesos on each to maintain account standing. The constraint set becomes:
\[
x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 50, \quad x_1 \geq 5, \quad x_2 \geq 5, \quad x_3 \geq 5
\]
Each inequality constraint \(x_k \geq 5\) gets its own multiplier \(\mu_k \geq 0\). The Lagrangian becomes:
\[
\mathcal{L} = f(x_1, x_2, x_3) - \lambda(x_1 + x_2 + x_3 - 50) + \sum_{k=1}^{3} \mu_k (x_k - 5)
\tag{4.8}\]
The sign convention here is deliberate. For inequality constraints of the form \(x_k \geq 5\), the multiplier \(\mu_k\) enters with a positive sign and is constrained to be non-negative. A binding constraint (\(x_k = 5\)) may have \(\mu_k > 0\); an inactive constraint (\(x_k > 5\)) forces \(\mu_k = 0\). This is complementary slackness ã a condition the next chapter treats in full.






TipWhat carries forward to SVR



The dual structure in Equationô 4.5 appears in every kernel model in this book. In Chapter 12, when we derive the SVR dual, the same construction appears: a Lagrangian with multipliers for each training constraint, a dual objective \(g(\boldsymbol{\alpha})\) that is a function of those multipliers, and strong duality guaranteeing that solving the dual gives the primal solution. The shadow price interpretation carries over too ã SVR multipliers encode which training points are on the margin.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 4ô  The Lagrangian and Duality
text:
4.5 Exercises
Exercise 4.1. Consider the problem of maximizing \(f(x_1, x_2) = \sqrt{x_1} + \sqrt{x_2}\) subject to \(x_1 + x_2 = 1\), \(x_1, x_2 \geq 0\).

Write the Lagrangian and derive the stationarity conditions.
Show that the optimum is \(x_1^* = x_2^* = 0.5\) and find the shadow price \(\lambda^*\).
Interpret \(\lambda^*\): what does an additional unit of total budget buy at the margin?

Exercise 4.2. The function \(h(x) = a(1 - e^{-bx})\) models conversions as a function of spend, with \(a > 0\) and \(b > 0\).

Show that \(h\) is concave for all \(x \geq 0\).
Suppose two channels have parameters \((a_1, b_1)\) and \((a_2, b_2)\). Write the equal-marginal-return condition for the two-channel budget allocation problem with total budget \(B\).
Show that if \(b_1 > b_2\) (channel 1 saturates faster), then the optimal allocation satisfies \(x_1^* < x_2^*\) when \(a_1 = a_2\).

Exercise 4.3. The dual objective in Equationô 4.5 is claimed to be convex in \(\lambda\).

Write \(g(\lambda) = \max_x \mathcal{L}(x, \lambda)\) and show that it is a pointwise maximum of affine functions of \(\lambda\).
Explain why the pointwise maximum of affine functions is convex.
Does strong duality depend on \(g\) being convex? Explain why or why not.

Exercise 4.4 (Graduate level). Consider the marketing problem with \(n\) channels and total budget \(B\). Suppose the conversion function for channel \(k\) is \(h_k(x_k) = a_k(1 - e^{-b_k x_k})\).

Derive the optimal allocation \(x_k^*\) as a function of \(\lambda^*\) and the channel parameters.
Show that the optimal allocation satisfies \(x_k^* = -\frac{1}{b_k} \log\!\left(\frac{\lambda^*}{a_k b_k}\right)\).
The constraint \(\sum_k x_k^* = B\) implicitly defines \(\lambda^*\). Show that this equation has a unique solution in \(\lambda^*\) and describe a bisection procedure to find it.
